retrieved_docs.title: Tricuspid Stenosis -- Evaluation -- Electrocardiogram
retrieved_docs.content: An electrocardiogram may show P-pulmonale—tall and peaked P waves—as evidence of right atrial enlargement. Tall and peaked T waves are mainly observed in leads II, III, and aVF. At least 50% of patients develop atrial fibrillation.

retrieved_docs.title: Tricuspid Atresia -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Clinicians may identify patients not diagnosed antenatally at the time of critical congenital heart disease screening with hypoxemia or the presence of a heart murmur. A chest x-ray may show decreased pulmonary vascular markings, which signify pulmonary oligemia. A prominent right heart border may also indicate right atrial dilation. The electrocardiogram (ECG) of the lesion is characterized by a left superior QRS axis (-30 to -90), representing a characteristic finding (see Image. ECG of a Patient With Tricuspid Atresia). Diminished right ventricular forces and potential evidence of left ventricular hypertrophy may also be observed. [15]

retrieved_docs.title: Tricuspid Atresia -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Exploring crucial insights and noteworthy considerations, this section delves into other significant aspects related to tricuspid atresia, including diagnostic clues, long-term outcomes, and historical milestones in Fontan palliation. A high index of suspicion is necessary for tricuspid atresia in newborns with cyanosis and left axis deviation or left ventricular hypertrophy on ECG. Patients with tricuspid atresia have a single left ventricle (systemic ventricle), resulting in the most favorable long-term outcomes among single ventricle cases undergoing the Fontan procedure. Among single ventricles, tricuspid atresia was the first type for which the Fontan palliation was performed in 1971.

retrieved_docs.title: Tricuspid Atresia -- Deterrence and Patient Education
retrieved_docs.content: In addition to the clinical assessment during yearly follow-ups, an annual ECG and transthoracic echocardiogram are necessary. A more comprehensive cardiovascular and extracardiac evaluation should be conducted every 3 to 4 years to monitor for the aforementioned complications. The comprehensive cardiovascular surveillance may involve a 24-hour Holter monitor, exercise stress test, pro-brain natriuretic peptide measurement, cardiac magnetic resonance imaging, computed tomography angiography, and cardiac catheterization. Organ system surveillance includes monitoring organ function through blood tests and extracardiac imaging. [34] [37]

retrieved_docs.title: Aortic Valvular Atresia -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: The initial electrocardiogram classically shows right axis deviation, right ventricular hypertrophy, and right atrial enlargement, though it can also show a pattern considered normal for patient age. This is generally attributed to the hypoplastic left side of the heart, but there are a few reported cases of left axis deviation and, rarely, left ventricular hypertrophy. There are occasionally problems with bundle branch conduction, and the Wolff-Parkinson-White pattern and the right bundle branch block have been reported. There is a reported lack of a Q wave in lead V6, which has been reported as a possible exclusion marker for aortic valvular atresia. The lack of this finding is not specific to aortic valvular atresia, as other congenital heart conditions or even normal infants may lack the Q-wave in this lead. T-wave depressions in leads I and II and ST-segment depressions in the left precordial leads have also frequently been reported. [5] [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Partial and Total Anomalous Pulmonary Venous Connection -- Evaluation -- Electrocardiography
retrieved_docs.content: Right axis deviation and right ventricular hypertrophy are often present. In TAPVC without obstruction tall peaked P waves in lead II, suggestive of right atrial enlargement may also be seen.

retrieved_docs.title: Cyanotic Heart Disease -- Evaluation -- Electrocardiogram (ECG)
retrieved_docs.content: ECG identifies rhythm abnormalities, auricular or ventricular hypertrophy, dextrocardias, or abnormal axis deviation. Chest X-Ray

retrieved_docs.title: Transposition of the Great Arteries -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Electrocardiography: Usually appears normal but may demonstrate right-axis deviation and right ventricular hypertrophy (RVH). Several criteria, primarily based on the R and S wave amplitudes in leads I, V1, and V6, along with the R-wave peak time in V1, have been established for diagnosing RVH. Chest radiography: The classic radiographic feature of TGA is the "egg on a string" appearance. Echocardiography: Transthoracic echocardiography is the primary imaging modality for TGA, providing essential information on the morphology, function, and hemodynamics of the ventricles and valves. [23] Cardiac catheterization: Angiography is rarely used to diagnose TGA but remains the gold standard for determining the origins of the coronary arteries. Cardiac catheterization is routinely utilized in d-TGA to perform balloon atrial septostomy in patients with severe cyanosis and may also assist in identifying coronary arteries. [24]

retrieved_docs.title: Ebstein Anomaly and Malformation -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: The most common physical finding in neonates and infants with Ebstein anomaly is cyanosis, present in about 50% of cases. [27] Even in the presence of tricuspid regurgitation and RV dysfunction, those without cyanosis are more likely to survive into early childhood. [5] Symptoms of heart failure in early childhood may be a prominent v wave in the jugular venous pulse from severe tricuspid regurgitation; this finding may be absent in torrential tricuspid regurgitation due to rapid equalization of pressures across the tricuspid valve. [28] There may be a loud first heart sound due to anterior leaflet closure, referred to as the "sail sound," and the first heart sound may be split due to delayed tricuspid valve closure. A holosystolic murmur of tricuspid regurgitation is another common physical finding in these patients. Multiple ejection clicks can be heard in the presence of a very mobile anterior leaflet. [13] [27]

retrieved_docs.title: Tricuspid Regurgitation -- Evaluation -- Chest Radiography
retrieved_docs.content: Electrocardiography: Nonspecific ST and T waves in the right precordial leads reflecting right ventricular dysfunction seen. If pulmonary hypertension causes tricuspid regurgitation, the electrocardiogram may show right axis deviation, indicating right ventricle hypertrophy and tall R waves in V1 to V2. In severe pulmonary hypertension, right atrial hypertrophy and P pulmonale may also be present. Cardiac catheterization: Elevated end-diastolic pressures in the right atrium and right ventricle are noted.

retrieved_docs.title: Tricuspid Atresia -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Echocardiography serves as a diagnostic tool for tricuspid atresia. Two-dimensional echocardiography would show the absence of the tricuspid valve and disproportionate sizes of the ventricular cavities, with the left ventricle larger than the right. Color flow Doppler demonstrates the lack of flow across the tricuspid valve (see Image. Echocardiography of a Patient With Tricuspid Atresia).

retrieved_docs.title: Tricuspid Atresia -- Deterrence and Patient Education
retrieved_docs.content: History and physical examination are beneficial in predicting many of the complications described above. Although mild jugular venous distension is typical following cavo-pulmonary anastomosis, marked jugular venous distension and hepatomegaly warrant additional assessment for potential obstruction in Glenn or Fontan procedures or systolic ventricular dysfunction of the heart. Edema and diarrhea may be present in the context of PLE. Palpitations in the medical history should trigger additional assessment for arrhythmias. Respiratory distress may indicate heart failure, while a history of thick airway secretions may suggest the presence of plastic bronchitis. A comprehensive history and physical examination during follow-up visits are essential for monitoring these patients effectively.

retrieved_docs.title: Tricuspid Atresia -- History and Physical -- Patients without Pulmonary Obstruction
retrieved_docs.content: Patients with a VSD and without PS often experience substantial pulmonary blood flow. Such cases could be missed at birth, as these individuals do not present with cyanosis. The condition may become apparent when pulmonary vascular resistance drops, leading to pulmonary overcirculation. This condition manifests with signs and symptoms of heart failure, such as tachypnea or respiratory distress, poor feeding, and impaired growth. Notable findings upon physical examination include tachypnea, tachycardia, and hepatomegaly in patients exhibiting pulmonary plethora. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Atrioventricular Septal Defect -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Patients with a partial AVSD without other complex congenital cardiac malformations and minimal AVV regurgitation usually remain asymptomatic in infancy and early childhood. They are diagnosed based on incidental findings, including pulmonary or tricuspid flow murmur and a fixed splitting due to an atrial septal defect. Symptoms of heart failure in infants may include difficulty in feeding, sleepiness, lethargy, and failure to thrive, while children may have a complaint of dyspnea. Signs of heart failure include: Tachypnea, tachycardia S3 gallop Rales on chest auscultation Raised jugular venous pressure Tender hepatomegaly Wide fixed splitting due to an atrial septal defect Pansystolic murmur due to AVV regurgitation Pulmonary flow murmur due to increased flow through the pulmonary valve Middiastolic flow murmur due to increased flow through the tricuspid valve

retrieved_docs.title: Pulmonary Regurgitation -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Electrocardiogram: While an ECG is unnecessary for diagnosing pulmonary regurgitation, this testing assesses potentially associated conditions, including arrhythmias and indications of right ventricle hypertrophy and congenital heart disease. Typically, the ECG reveals sinus rhythm, although arrhythmias can develop, as mentioned earlier. Patients who have undergone repaired tetralogy of Fallot with longstanding severe pulmonary regurgitation often exhibit right bundle branch block with QRS prolongation. Right axis deviation and criteria for right ventricle hypertrophy may be evident in patients with concomitant right ventricle outflow tract obstruction or pulmonary arterial hypertension. [26] Obtaining an ECG aids in identifying these additional findings, contributing to the comprehensive evaluation of patients with pulmonary regurgitation.

retrieved_docs.title: Tricuspid Regurgitation -- Consultations
retrieved_docs.content: Consultations that may be needed to evaluate tricuspid regurgitation include the following: Cardiologist for evaluation and medical management Cardiac surgeon if the patient is symptomatic and may need surgical evaluation or management Cardiac anesthesia consults for preoperative monitoring and management

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 1862
retrieved_docs.content: Electrocardiographic and Monitoring Procedures is a comprehensive diagnostic category that encompasses several specialized techniques. Within this category, Cardiotachometry is used to assess heart rate and rhythm through pulse‑based measurements. Intracardiac phonocardiogram provides acoustic recordings from within the cardiac chambers to evaluate heart sounds directly. Monitoring of pulse is a fundamental component for evaluating circulatory status and detecting abnormalities in heart rhythm. Phonocardiogram with apex cardiogram records heart sounds at the cardiac apex, offering detailed information about valvular function and cardiac mechanics. Phonocardiogram with artery and/or vein tracing combines heart‑sound analysis with simultaneous arterial or venous waveform monitoring, allowing integrated assessment of cardiac and vascular dynamics. Together, these procedures constitute the full spectrum of electrocardiographic and monitoring methods employed in cardiac evaluation and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 2 -- Community 1148
retrieved_docs.content: valve pathologies, broader cardiac disease categories, diagnostic modalities and therapeutic interventions is essential for accurate diagnosis, optimal treatment planning and comprehensive patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1723
retrieved_docs.content: The clinical association between اضطراب النظام (systemic disorder) and تدلي الصمام المترالي (mitral valve prolapse) indicates that patients who exhibit systemic dysregulation may have an increased likelihood of developing mitral valve prolapse, a condition in which the mitral valve leaflets bulge into the left atrium during systole. Recognizing this link helps clinicians consider valvular assessment in individuals with systemic disorders, facilitating earlier diagnosis and more comprehensive management of potential cardiac involvement.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 2 -- Community 1149
retrieved_docs.content: Rheumatic fever (الحُمَّى الروماتيزمية) can lead to severe valvular damage that frequently requires heart‑valve operations, including aortic valve replacement (استبدال الصمام الأبهري), mitral (coronary) valve replacement (استبدال الصمام التاجي), tricuspid valve replacement, pulmonary valve replacement, transcatheter aortic valve implantation, and other aortic or mitral valve surgeries. These interventions are performed within the cardiovascular system, which also serves as the primary anatomic focus for a broad range of diagnostic, monitoring, and therapeutic procedures. Electrocardiographic and Monitoring Procedures, a subcategory of Cardiovascular and Respiratory System Procedures, encompass blood‑pressure monitoring, cardiotachometer monitoring, cardiotachometry, pacemaker therapy monitoring, routine pulse monitoring, and Holter monitor monitoring (the latter linked to unstable angina). Acoustic evaluations such as intracardiac phonocardiograms, phonocardiograms with apex

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2852
retrieved_docs.content: In clinical assessment, results that are above the reference range are classified as increased findings, and an increased finding is interpreted as indicating tachycardia (تسرع القلب).

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 1 -- Community 185
retrieved_docs.content: structural heart changes, electrophysiologic abnormalities, diagnostic terminologies, monitoring technologies, interventional procedures, pharmacologic considerations, and systemic organ interactions essential for accurate diagnosis, risk stratification, and holistic cardiac patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 1 -- Community 167
retrieved_docs.content: قلبية) comprise three evaluation formats: electrophysiologic evaluation with arrhythmia induction, arrhythmogenic focus ablation, and treatment of atrial fibrillation; the same format applied to supraventricular tachycardia; and the same format applied to ventricular tachycardia. The ventricular‑tachycardia evaluation emphasizes ventricular acceleration (التسارع البطيني), described as an automatic ventricular rhythm disorder (نظْمٌ بُطَينِيٌّ ذاتِيٌّ مُعَجَّل). Excision of a heart valve is placed under HEART: EXCISIONS and includes child procedures within that excision hierarchy. Collectively, these relationships delineate a structured taxonomy of cardiac and pericardial operative and diagnostic procedures, illustrating how broad categories subdivide into precise interventions such as valvotomy, pericardial excision, various biopsies, electrophysiological studies, papillary‑muscle operations, valve excisions, and temporary mechanical circulatory support, thereby aiding clinicians in

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 1 -- Community 181
retrieved_docs.content: in patients with atrial fibrillation. Electrophysiologic evaluation—comprising arrhythmia induction, arrhythmogenic focus ablation, and treatment of atrial fibrillation, supraventricular tachycardia, or ventricular tachycardia—constitutes cardiac electrophysiology studies that both diagnose and treat ventricular tachycardia (التسارع البطيني) by using induced arrhythmias to locate the arrhythmogenic substrate and applying targeted ablation. ECG monitoring devices (“ECG Monitors, Arrhythmia”) and non‑sinus rhythm ECG interpretations are employed to detect these conduction and rhythm disorders. All of these entities are organized under the higher‑level category HEART RATE AND RHYTHM DISORDERS, which encompasses heart‑rate concepts (معدل ضربات القلب) and links to neonatal cardiac assessment terminology (Neonatal Research Network Terminology), illustrating the interconnected nature of electrical propagation abnormalities, rapid heart‑rate disorders, their anatomical and autonomic

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 5 -- Community 2947
retrieved_docs.content: CARDIAC CONDUCTION DISEASE WITH OR WITHOUT DILATED CARDIOMYOPATHY can manifest as اعتلال عضلة القلب الاحتقاني, and this form of congestive cardiomyopathy is clinically associated with تَسَارُّعُ فَوقَ البُطَينِيّ, indicating that patients with this conduction disorder may develop congestive cardiomyopathy and present with supraventricular tachycardia.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2833
retrieved_docs.content: Cardiac dysrhythmias, which are classified as a subcategory of تَسَارُّعُ فَوقَ البُطَينِيّ (supraventricular tachycardia), are clinically associated with تضخم البطين الأيسر (left ventricular hypertrophy). This connection suggests that individuals experiencing cardiac dysrhythmias often show structural changes in the left ventricle, underscoring the need to assess ventricular hypertrophy when diagnosing and treating supraventricular tachyarrhythmias.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 2254
retrieved_docs.content: Electrocardiographic and Monitoring Procedures constitute a comprehensive category of diagnostic and therapeutic monitoring techniques that encompass several specific modalities. Blood pressure monitoring and cardiotachometry are identified as child procedures within this category, reflecting their roles in assessing circulatory dynamics and cardiac rate respectively. Additional child procedures include monitoring of pacemaker regime or therapy, which evaluates the function and settings of implanted cardiac devices; monitoring of pulse, which provides real‑time assessment of arterial pulse characteristics; and phonocardiogram with artery and/or vein tracing, which records acoustic heart sounds together with vascular flow patterns. Together, these interconnected procedures enable clinicians to obtain detailed cardiovascular information, guide treatment decisions, and ensure optimal patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 3 -- Community 2007
retrieved_docs.content: Various monitoring modalities—including “Electrocardiogram monitoring, 10 hour portrait,” “Fetal electrocardiogram,” “Monitoring of ECG at surgery,” and “Transesophageal echocardiographic monitoring”—all share the heart (srdce) as their direct procedural site, indicating that these diagnostic and intra‑operative techniques are performed directly on cardiac tissue. The heart (srdce) is also linked to the clinical entity “نفخة قلبية” (cardiac murmur), which is described as a child concept of the heart, reflecting that murmurs are considered a subordinate finding within cardiac assessment. Together, these relationships highlight that ECG‑based and echocardiographic monitoring procedures target the heart itself, and that cardiac murmurs represent a related cardiac condition relevant to patient evaluation and care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1776
retrieved_docs.content: CARDIAC CONDUCTION DISEASE WITH OR WITHOUT DILATED CARDIOMYOPATHY is characterized by the manifestation of cardiac dysrhythmias and also by the manifestation of اعتلال عضلة القلب الاحتقاني (congestive cardiomyopathy). The cardiac dysrhythmias that occur in this condition are clinically associated with تضخم البطين الأيسر (left ventricular hypertrophy), frequently co‑occur with رفرفة أذينية (atrial flutter), and are categorized under the parent rhythm disorder تَسَارُّعُ فَوقَ البُطَينِيّ (supraventricular tachycardia). Likewise, اعتلال عضلة القلب الاحتقاني shows clinical associations with تَسَارُّعُ فَوقَ البُطَينِيّ, رفرفة أذينية, and تضخم البطين الأيسر, indicating a close interrelationship among these electrophysiological and structural cardiac abnormalities. Recognizing these linked manifestations and associations is important for comprehensive evaluation and management of patients with cardiac conduction disease and related cardiomyopathic changes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2576
retrieved_docs.content: Neonatal Research Network Terminology incorporates the concept تَسَرُّعُ القَلْبِ الأُذَينِيّ المنتبذ, a specific term used to describe an accelerated or exuberant atrial cardiac activity in neonates. Inclusion of this term within the Neonatal Research Network Terminology provides a standardized label for this neonatal cardiac condition, supporting consistent documentation, research classification, and communication among clinicians and investigators who study or treat newborns with atypical atrial heart rhythms.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 4 -- Community 2888
retrieved_docs.content: Heart rate and rhythm disorders are classified as a subset of conduction disorders, indicating that these arrhythmic conditions arise from abnormalities in the cardiac electrical conduction system.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 3 -- Community 2326
retrieved_docs.content: Electrocardiographic and Monitoring Procedures is a broad category that incorporates several specific monitoring techniques. Blood pressure monitoring and cardiotachometer monitoring are identified as child procedures within this category, indicating they are specialized forms of electrocardiographic and monitoring assessment. In addition, the category includes intracardiac phonocardiogram, monitoring of pulse, and phonocardiogram with artery and/or vein tracing as subordinate procedures. Together, these modalities provide a comprehensive evaluation of cardiac electrical activity, hemodynamics, and vascular flow, supporting detailed patient assessment and management.
